breakClass: DateTime arithmetic helper inside an unmaterialized query: DbFunctionsCompat.AddDays/AddMinutes (plain CLR helpers in Helpers/SendMailShim.cs, NOT registered EF functions) applied to a COLUMN expression inside a server-side LINQ-to-Entities projection or Where. EF6 used DbFunctions.AddDays (canonical -> SQL DATEADD); the port swapped in DbFunctionsCompat.* which EF Core 10 cannot translate, so the query throws 'could not be translated' -> HTTP 500. Fix = EF-native (col).Value.AddDays(n ?? 0) which DOES translate to DATEADD.
why: select new SerialNoObj of unmaterialized BatchStocks join query; .ToList() boundary at L8508 after projection -> server-side. The ternary false-branch DbFunctionsCompat.AddDays(c.PEDate, ...) is a CLR helper -> whole projection untranslatable -> 500. Legacy (archive L9114) DbFunctions.AddDays. Port regression.
finalFix:
Apply at BOTH L8504 (BatchStock) and L8524 (BatchStockNw) in Controllers/PurchaseEntryController.cs. Replace the DbFunctionsCompat.AddDays call with an EF-native DateTime.AddDays on the non-nullable PEDate column:

ExpiryDate = (a.EXP != null) ? a.EXP : c.PEDate.AddDays((double)((d.daysexpirty != null) ? d.daysexpirty.Value : 360)),

Rationale: PEDate is non-nullable DateTime so no .Value (the hunter's `.Value` would not compile). daysexpirty is long? so use `.Value` when non-null else literal 360, cast to double for DateTime.AddDays(double). EF Core 10 translates DateTime.AddDays to SQL DATEADD(day, ...), producing identical output to legacy EF6 DbFunctions.AddDays(c.PEDate, n). The non-nullable DateTime result implicitly widens to the ternary's DateTime? type (SerialNoObj.ExpiryDate). This removes the untranslatable CLR helper and restores server-side translation, eliminating the 500.
reasoning:
I independently confirmed BOTH conditions.

(a) SERVER-SIDE: In GenerateSerialNo(long id, long? ItemId) (Controllers/PurchaseEntryController.cs), the `BatchStock` query (L8495-8508) is built from three DbSets (db.BatchStocks join db.PurchaseEntrys join db.Items) with `select new SerialNoObj { ... }` composed INTO the IQueryable. The FIRST materialization boundary is `.ToList()` at L8508, which is DOWNSTREAM of the projection. So the projection (including the DbFunctionsCompat.AddDays(c.PEDate, ...) false-branch of the ExpiryDate ternary at L8504) is part of the SQL translation -> genuinely server-side. The identical pattern repeats in `BatchStockNw` at L8524 (.ToList() at L8528). Neither is after a materialization boundary, neither is a group-aggregate over an IGrouping.

(b) WOULD 500: DbFunctionsCompat.AddDays (Helpers/SendMailShim.cs L70: `public static DateTime? AddDays(DateTime? date, int? days) => date?.AddDays(days ?? 0);`) is a plain CLR static method â€” NOT a [DbFunction] and NOT an EF.Functions extension, so EF Core 10 has no translation for it. When EF Core builds the SQL for this projection it hits the unmapped method call and throws InvalidOperationException 'The LINQ expression ... could not be translated' at query-compile time. With EF Core's default (no implicit client eval of arbitrary projection fragments since 3.0), this is an unhandled exception -> HTTP 500. It fires whenever ItemId != null on this action, BEFORE any row evaluation, so it is data-independent -> HIGH severity (not edge-only).

PORT REGRESSION confirmed against legacy: archive L9114 used `DbFunctions.AddDays(c.PEDate, ...)` (System.Data.Entity.DbFunctions canonical -> SQL DATEADD, worked server-side in EF6). The port swapped in the untranslatable DbFunctionsCompat.* helper. Pure port regression.

NOTE / correction to the hunter's proposed fix: PurchaseEntry.PEDate is NON-nullable `DateTime` (Models/PurchaseEntry.cs L14), not DateTime?. So the hunter's `c.PEDate.Value.AddDays(...)` would NOT compile (no .Value on a non-nullable DateTime). Correct form calls .AddDays directly on PEDate. SerialNoObj.ExpiryDate is DateTime? (ViewModel/PurchaseEntryViewModel.cs L198) and Item.daysexpirty is long? (Models/Item.cs L43), so the non-nullable DateTime result widens to DateTime? in the ternary â€” type-compatible and output-preserving. EF Core 10 natively translates DateTime.AddDays(double) to SQL DATEADD, exactly mirroring legacy EF6 DbFunctions.AddDays.

finalFix:
Replace the untranslatable CLR helper with EF-native DateTime.AddDays so EF Core 10 emits SQL DATEADD (mirroring legacy EF6 DbFunctions.AddDays). PEDate is non-nullable DateTime, so call .AddDays directly on it; the days operand is long? so take .Value (AddDays accepts the double conversion). At line 8524 (BatchStockNw):
  ExpiryDate = (a.EXP != null) ? a.EXP : (DateTime?)c.PEDate.AddDays((double)((d.daysexpirty != null) ? d.daysexpirty.Value : 360)),

Apply the identical fix to the sibling BatchStock query at line 8504:
  ExpiryDate = (a.EXP != null) ? a.EXP : (DateTime?)c.PEDate.AddDays((double)((d.daysexpirty != null) ? d.daysexpirty.Value : 360)),

Notes: c.PEDate.AddDays(n) translates to DATEADD(day, n, PEDate) in EF Core 10, exactly reproducing legacy DbFunctions.AddDays output. The hunter's proposed `c.PEDate.Value.AddDays(...)` is WRONG here â€” PEDate is non-nullable DateTime and has no .Value member, so that would not compile; use c.PEDate.AddDays(...) directly. The (double) cast (or .Value on the long?) is required because daysexpirty is long? and AddDays takes a double.
reasoning:
I tried to refute but the claim holds on both prongs.

(a) SERVER-SIDE â€” confirmed. The flagged expression at Controllers/PurchaseEntryController.cs:8524 lives inside the `select new SerialNoObj { ... }` projection of the `BatchStockNw` IQueryable (lines 8515-8528). The `.ToList()` at L8528 is the materialization boundary, but it ENUMERATES this query â€” the projection (including the AddDays call) is the thing EF Core must translate to SQL. There is no `.ToList()`/`.AsEnumerable()`/`.ToArray()` boundary anywhere inside the query before the `select`; the three joins (BatchStocks/PurchaseEntrys/Items) and the `where` are all composed into one unmaterialized IQueryable. This is not a standalone client-side local, not a group-aggregate over an IGrouping. Genuinely server-side.

(b) WOULD 500 â€” confirmed. DbFunctionsCompat.AddDays is a plain static CLR helper in Helpers/SendMailShim.cs:70-71 (`public static DateTime? AddDays(DateTime? date, int? days) => date?.AddDays(days ?? 0);`) â€” explicitly commented "EF Core has no EF.Functions.AddDays; client-evaluated", NOT registered as an EF function via HasDbFunction. When EF Core 10 translates the projection it hits this unmapped method and throws InvalidOperationException "could not be translated" -> HTTP 500. The failure happens at translation time (the `.ToList()` enumeration), so it is data-independent: any request that reaches this code with a non-null ItemId throws, even with zero matching rows. High severity.

PORT REGRESSION â€” confirmed against legacy. _archive/legacy-mvc5/Controllers/PurchaseEntryController.cs:9134 is byte-for-byte the same query but uses `DbFunctions.AddDays(c.PEDate, ...)` â€” the EF6 canonical function that maps to SQL DATEADD and translated fine. The port swapped DbFunctions.AddDays -> DbFunctionsCompat.AddDays, breaking translation. Same regression also exists at the sibling BatchStock query L8504 (legacy L9114) and at GenerateSerialNo-area queries L9276/L9292 in legacy.

TYPES verified: PurchaseEntry.PEDate is non-nullable `DateTime` (Models/PurchaseEntry.cs:14); BatchStock.EXP is `DateTime?` (Models/BatchStock.cs:13); Item.daysexpirty is `long?` (ViewModel/ItemViewModel.cs:66). ExpiryDate target is DateTime? (matches the `a.EXP` true-branch). Overload bound is AddDays(DateTime?,int?) returning DateTime?.

In Controllers/PurchaseEntryController.cs, replace the untranslatable CLR helper with EF-native, translatable DateTime arithmetic mirroring legacy DATEADD, in BOTH projection blocks of GetSerialNoDtlInCreateMode. PEDate is non-nullable DateTime (so no .Value) and daysexpirty is long?.

Line 8661 (Items/PEItemss block):
  ExpiryDate = c.PEDate.AddDays((double)(a.daysexpirty ?? 360)),

Line 8677 (Items/DummyPEItems2 block):
  ExpiryDate = c.PEDate.AddDays((double)(a.daysexpirty ?? 360)),

Rationale: EF Core 10 translates DateTime.AddDays(double) to SQL DATEADD, exactly reproducing legacy EF6 DbFunctions.AddDays(c.PEDate, daysexpirty|360). `a.daysexpirty ?? 360` preserves the legacy default-of-360-when-null semantics; casting to double selects the translatable AddDays(double) overload. Output (the ExpiryDate value) is identical to legacy. The result type changes from DateTime? to non-nullable DateTime, which serializes the same in the JSON response. (Same swap should also be applied to the identical sibling occurrences flagged by grep at PurchaseEntryController.cs:8504/8524 and PurchaseEntryExpenseController.cs:8091/8111/8248, plus the other DbFunctionsCompat.AddDays validity-expression sites in MyReports/Quotation/PurchaseOrder/etc., but those are out of scope for this specific finding.)

finalFix:
Replace the untranslatable CLR helper with an EF-native DateTime.AddDays(double), which EF Core 10 translates to SQL DATEADD, preserving the legacy DbFunctions.AddDays(DATEADD) behavior. PEDate is non-nullable DateTime and daysexpirty is long?, so:

And apply the SAME fix to the primary branch at line 8661 (same bug, will 500 first):
  ExpiryDate = c.PEDate.AddDays((double)(a.daysexpirty ?? 360)),

This is output-preserving: legacy added (daysexpirty ?? 360) days to PEDate via DATEADD; this produces the identical DateTime. (If matching the original's nullable result type is desired for the JSON shape, cast the column: `ExpiryDate = (DateTime?)c.PEDate.AddDays((double)(a.daysexpirty ?? 360))` â€” but since PEDate is non-nullable the value is never null anyway.)

I attempted to refute but could not. The flag is real.

(a) SERVER-SIDE â€” confirmed. In GetSerialNoDtlInCreateMode (Controllers/PurchaseEntryController.cs L8648-8682), the fallback DummyPEItems2 branch builds `(from a in db.Items join b in db.DummyPEItems2 ... join c in db.PurchaseEntrys ... select new { ... ExpiryDate = DbFunctionsCompat.AddDays(c.PEDate, (int?)((a.daysexpirty != null) ? a.daysexpirty : 360)), ... })` and the `.ToList()` materialization boundary is at L8679 â€” AFTER the projection. So the projection (including the AddDays call) is composed into the IQueryable and handed to the EF Core 10 SQL translator. Not a post-materialization fragment, not a standalone local, not an IGrouping aggregate. The identical primary branch at L8661/L8663 has the same shape.

(b) WOULD 500 â€” confirmed. DbFunctionsCompat.AddDays is a plain CLR static method (Helpers/SendMailShim.cs L70: `public static DateTime? AddDays(DateTime? date, int? days) => date?.AddDays(days ?? 0);`) â€” it is NOT a [DbFunction]-registered/canonical function. EF Core 10 cannot translate an arbitrary user static-method call inside a server-side projection, throwing InvalidOperationException 'The LINQ expression ... could not be translated' â†’ HTTP 500. This fires on ANY row the fallback query returns (high severity, not just edge data), since the column ExpiryDate is materialized for every row.

PORT REGRESSION â€” confirmed against _archive\legacy-mvc5\Controllers\PurchaseEntryController.cs L9292, which used `DbFunctions.AddDays(c.PEDate, (int?)((a.daysexpirty != null) ? a.daysexpirty : 360))`. EF6's DbFunctions.AddDays is a canonical function mapping to SQL DATEADD and translated fine. The port swapped in the untranslatable CLR helper. So EF6 worked, EF Core 10 breaks â€” genuine regression.

CORRECTION to the proposed fix: the hunter's `c.PEDate.Value.AddDays(...)` is WRONG. PurchaseEntry.PEDate is non-nullable `DateTime` (Models/PurchaseEntry.cs L14 & L127), so `.Value` does not exist and won't compile. Also Item.daysexpirty is `long?` (Models/Item.cs L43), not int?, so `(a.daysexpirty ?? 360).Value` is also wrong shape. The correct EF-native form uses DateTime.AddDays(double) which EF Core translates to DATEADD.

NOTE: the primary branch at L8661 has the identical bug; if only the fallback (L8677) is patched, the primary branch will still 500 first. Both lines should be fixed identically.
